text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
Riverside Medical Practice  
14 Brookside Road, London NW1 5DL  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Alex Morgan, DOB: 22-07-1990, Hospital No: U394720 NHS No. 1029384756  
Flat 3, 27 Alder Street, London E1 8DN 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Seizure disorder with prior event during commercial flight/health anxiety focused on travel/sleep disruption around journeys/migraine without aura/GERD. 

Medication: Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.  
Propranolol 20 mg as needed for situational anxiety (patient-reported use prior to flights).  
Omeprazole 20 mg once daily.  
Sumatriptan 50 mg as needed for migraine (max 2 doses in 24 hours).  

I reviewed Alex today in clinic. They attended alone and were calm and engaged throughout. They describe working full-time in IT support and living with their partner. Since our last review, they have resumed short-haul train trips for work and report that this has gone well. They continue to experience anticipatory anxiety specifically related to flying, which they link to the in-flight event in May this year. Sleep is often curtailed the night before travel. Appetite is stable and weight unchanged. No new neurological complaints between episodes. Cardiovascular and respiratory assessments in primary care after the flight incident were unremarkable. 

Seizures: Alex keeps a digital log. They report no events in June, July, August, or September 2025. The most recent episode occurred on 17 May 2025 while on an overnight flight from Lisbon to London. As per fellow passenger and cabin crew accounts, the event began with a brief period of behavioural pause and lip-smacking, followed by loss of awareness and sustained bilateral stiffening with rhythmic movements, lasting approximately 90 seconds, with tongue biting and post-event confusion for around 20 minutes. This pattern is consistent with a history of episodes that appear to start focally and then generalise to a convulsive phase. There is no consistent warning beyond a rising internal unease, and no bowel or bladder incontinence reported. No confirmed triggers other than sleep deprivation and heightened travel-related anxiety. No further events since. 

Travel/anxiety context: Alex recognises avoidance behaviours around air travel and is open to structured support. They report that practising breathing techniques and ensuring a clear pre-flight sleep plan have been helpful. They decline alcohol before and during flights. They understand airline medical procedures and have informed their insurer of their condition. 

Examination today: Neurological examination was normal. Gait steady. No focal deficits. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations within normal limits. 

Investigations: MRI brain (Nov 2023) reported normal. Routine EEG (Jan 2024) non-diagnostic. Bloods (U&E, LFT, FBC) in July 2025 within reference ranges. 

Plan:  
- Continue current lamotrigine dose; no adjustment at present given four months’ stability and good tolerability.  
- Provide a personalised written travel plan outlining sleep hygiene, hydration, and stepwise responses if warning features occur while travelling.  
- Offer referral to clinical psychology for time-limited sessions targeting travel-related anxiety and pre-travel sleep.  
- Supply a fit-to-fly letter for the patient to carry, detailing rescue advice and post-event care for lay responders.  
- Recheck routine safety bloods at next review or sooner if clinically indicated.  
- Follow-up in 6 months, or earlier if breakthrough events occur.  

Yours sincerely

entities.text: They report no events in June, July, August, or September 2025. The most recent episode occurred on 17 May 2025.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Clinic date is 02 Oct 2025. Letter states no events in June, July, August, or September 2025, and the most recent seizure was on 17 May 2025 with no further events since. This is fewer than 6 months seizure-free, so do not label as seizure free. Calculate frequency from the explicit interval May–September (5 months) with 1 seizure, yielding approximately 1 per 5 months.
seizure_frequency_number: They report no events in June, July, August, or September 2025. The most recent episode occurred on 17 May 2025.
reference: Seizure events suspected focal to bilateral tonic-clonic

flaws.description: Gold (1 per 5 month) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
Row marked as not OK in raw dataset
Quote issue flagged for seizure frequency
